question: Should "genie" — a harness-agnostic MCP App that clones Anthropic's Claude Design (an AI UI-component generator that renders a live preview + design-system/UI-kit browser inside AI coding harnesses like Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code via MCP) — adopt the Skybridge open-source React framework (https://www.skybridge.tech/, "the full-stack React framework for MCP apps and MCP servers", MIT-licensed, by MCP Apps spec contributors)? Research these angles: (1) Skybridge's actual technical architecture and capabilities — how it builds MCP Apps, its type-safe tRPC-style server-tool-to-React-view binding, devtools (local emulator, HMR, public tunnel, server audit), Skills/CLI/agent APIs, and what an MCP App built with it actually looks like in code. (2) Does its "write once, run everywhere" cross-harness claim actually deliver — real parity and limitations across Claude Desktop, ChatGPT, Cursor, VS Code, Goose, and the MCP Apps display-modes (inline/fullscreen/pip)? (3) Skybridge vs the hand-rolled alternative (raw MCP SDK + Vite + ui:// resources + iframe, or mcp-ui) — tradeoffs, lock-in, the Alpic deployment dependency, and whether the embedded-tier CSP constraints (default-src none, no web fonts) are handled. (4) Maturity and risk — verify the "powering 10% of MCP apps", 100K monthly npm downloads, 1K+ GitHub stars claims; production-readiness; governance; how tied it is to the still-draft MCP Apps extension. (5) Concrete fit for genie specifically — would a two-surface app (live component preview grid + UI-kit file browser) with refine/generate flows benefit, and what would genie gain or lose vs its current plan. Conclude with a clear adopt / partially-adopt / don't-adopt recommendation with reasoning.
summary: Recommendation: PARTIALLY ADOPT (adopt-with-guardrails). Skybridge is a genuine MIT-licensed full-stack React/TypeScript framework — not a thin SDK wrapper — offering tRPC-style type-safe binding from MCP server tool definitions to React views, plus real local DevTools (localhost:3000), instant HMR, and a `--tunnel` public URL + live LLM playground; its core npm package draws ~97K downloads/30d (≈53K genuine project scaffolds), and Alpic hosting is optional (self-host on any Node.js platform). The decisive structural fact for genie is that Skybridge is *syntactic sugar over a standard genie could implement directly*: the MCP Apps extension (SEP-1865) standardizes UI as predeclared `ui://` resources (mimeType `text/html+mcp`) referenced by tool metadata, rendered in sandboxed iframes, communicating via JSON-RPC over postMessage — buildable with the raw `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, and equally served by the vendor-neutral, now-same-lineage `mcp-ui` SDK. This means lock-in risk is low (you can eject to raw `ui://` later) while the velocity gains (type-safe binding, devtools, tunnel testing) are real and directly useful for genie's refine/generate flows. The main reasons to only *partially* commit: the "write once, run everywhere" cross-harness promise is an ecosystem aspiration the spec explicitly refuses to guarantee (hosts adopt at their own pace; text fallback recommended), Skybridge's own docs name only ChatGPT/Claude/VSCode (not Cursor or Goose), and several question-critical items — embedded-tier CSP handling (`default-src none`, no web fonts), inline/fullscreen/pip display-mode parity, the "~10% of MCP apps" and "1K+ stars" marketing claims — went unverified. Prototype genie on Skybridge for speed, but keep the `ui://`+iframe escape hatch open and independently validate cross-harness parity and CSP constraints before betting genie's harness-agnostic identity on it.

findings.claim: Skybridge is an MIT-licensed full-stack TypeScript/React framework providing tRPC-style type inference that binds MCP server tool definitions to React views (typed hooks like useToolInfo<"searchFlights">(), declarative view config), plus a real local dev experience: in-browser DevTools at localhost:3000 to test tools/preview views/switch themes+locales, instant hot-reload, and a --tunnel flag yielding a public URL + live LLM playground for real MCP clients. No 'server audit' feature is documented.
findings.evidence: Two primary sources (GitHub repo + skybridge.tech) confirm MIT license and verbatim 'tRPC-style inference from MCP server tool definition to React view for type safety from server to frontend,' with concrete API evidence (typed hooks, view:{component:...} registration, server schema changes surfacing instantly as widget typecheck errors) — establishing it is more than a raw SDK wrapper. Primary docs confirm DevTools(:3000), HMR ('View changes hot-reload instantly'), and `npm run dev -- --tunnel` → 'a live LLM playground and a public URL for ChatGPT, Claude, or any MCP client.' The negative ('no server audit') was checked against the full /llms.txt docs index where 'audit' appears nowhere; the question explicitly asked about a server-audit devtool, so its documented absence is material. Caveat: WebSearch was degraded during verification, so corroboration rests on the (authoritative) primary docs.

findings.claim: The cross-harness 'write once, run everywhere' premise is real as a standard direction but parity is NOT guaranteed: ChatGPT implements the open MCP Apps standard so you can 'build once and run across MCP Apps-compatible hosts,' yet this is qualified (window.openai is a ChatGPT-only compatibility/extension layer, not baseline; broad compatibility also requires setting _meta.ui.resourceUri), and the MCP Apps spec itself makes no write-once guarantee — its motivation is to reduce divergence/fragmentation, with hosts adopting UI support at their own pace and text-only fallback recommended.
findings.evidence: OpenAI's first-party Apps SDK docs verbatim: 'build once and run your UI across MCP Apps-compatible hosts,' while explicitly labeling window.openai an 'Apps SDK compatibility layer' you 'shouldn't depend on for baseline MCP Apps compatibility,' and instructing 'also set _meta.ui.resourceUri on the render tool.' The official MCP Apps announcement never asserts uniform behavior; it frames the goal as preventing 'a real risk of ecosystem fragmentation,' notes 'hosts can gradually adopt UI support at their own pace,' and that servers 'should provide text-only fallback.' Together these temper any vendor parity marketing: the standard enables portability but does not promise identical rendering/behavior across harnesses.

findings.claim: Skybridge is syntactic sugar over a standard genie could implement directly, so deployment/lock-in risk is low. The hand-rolled stack (iframe + predeclared ui:// resources + postMessage bridge) is exactly what ChatGPT's host and the MCP Apps spec expect: UI is modeled as ui:// resources (mimeType text/html+mcp) referenced by tool metadata, run in sandboxed iframes, communicating via JSON-RPC 2.0 over postMessage, and buildable with the standard @modelcontextprotocol/sdk. Skybridge's Alpic hosting is optional — apps self-host on any Node.js-compatible platform.
findings.evidence: OpenAI docs: components 'run inside an iframe,' 'talk to the host via the MCP Apps bridge (JSON-RPC over postMessage),' templates are 'MCP resources referenced by ui:// URIs.' The official MCP Apps spec/SEP confirms 'UI templates are resources with the ui:// URI scheme,' example mimeType 'text/html+mcp,' 'All UI content runs in sandboxed iframes,' and 'developers can use the standard @modelcontextprotocol/sdk.' Skybridge's own README states 'You can also self-host on any Node.js-compatible platform,' framing Alpic as one option not a requirement. Implication: the underlying primitives are standardized, so genie retains a low-cost ejection path from Skybridge to raw ui://. Medium-confidence sub-point: self-host steps are deferred to an external guide (practical completeness unverified).

findings.claim: mcp-ui is a viable vendor-neutral alternative to Skybridge with no managed-deployment dependency, and it is now the SAME lineage as the official standard rather than a competitor. It is an MIT-licensed SDK (@mcp-ui/server + @mcp-ui/client + standard @modelcontextprotocol/sdk) that binds tools to UI via ui:// resources and _meta.ui.resourceUri, self-positions as production-ready, and was standardized/absorbed into MCP Apps (SEP-1865 authored by mcp-ui creator Ido Salomon).
findings.evidence: mcp-ui README: 'SDK implementing the MCP Apps standard for UI over MCP,' 'fully compliant... ready for production use,' host-agnostic adapters that 'work seamlessly regardless of host,' library packages with no hosted-platform requirement. mcpui.dev: 'MCP-UI is now standardized into MCP Apps!' and 'The MCP-UI packages implement the spec'; binding shown via createUIResource + _meta.ui.resourceUri, ui:// scheme, sandboxed iframes. SEP-1865 (merged Final, Jan 28 2026) was authored by idosal himself and unifies the MCP-UI and Apps SDK approaches — confirming same lineage. Caveats: 'production-ready' is self-asserted (README carries a 'use at your own risk' disclaimer; mcpui.dev omits the production claim), and mcp-ui is a superset/reference implementation that also keeps a legacy postMessage path.

findings.claim: MCP Apps (the standard Skybridge depends on) is now finalized but young: it was announced 2025-11-21 as an intentionally-lean, OPTIONAL extension with only an 'early access SDK,' and SEP-1865 is now marked Status 'Final' on the Extensions Track (created 2025-11-21, merged Jan 28 2026). The research question's 'still-draft' framing is outdated, but the extension remains lean and recently-finalized — a maturity risk any dependent framework inherits.
findings.evidence: PR #1865 metadata (authoritative spec repo): 'SEP-1865: MCP Apps,' Track: Extensions, Status: Final, Created: 2025-11-21, merged 2026-01-28 with labels extension/final/SEP — so it is NOT a draft. The Nov-21 announcement candidly self-describes the proposal as 'intentionally lean, starting with core patterns that we plan on expanding over time,' 'an optional extension,' with 'an early access SDK to demonstrate the patterns.' Reconciliation: it was early/lean at announcement and is now Final-status but still a young, deliberately-minimal optional extension expected to expand — i.e. finalized core, evolving surface.

findings.claim: Adoption metrics partially substantiate Skybridge's marketing but warrant skepticism. The core 'skybridge' npm package recorded 97,332 downloads in the last 30 days (2026-05-23 to 2026-06-21), matching the '~100K monthly downloads' claim within ~3%; however that headline belongs to the core package (inflated by CI/transitive pulls — 90-day core:scaffolder ratio ~2:1), while the better proxy for genuinely new projects is the create-skybridge scaffolder at 52,929/30d (~53K). The separately-marketed '~10% of all MCP apps' and '1K+ GitHub stars' claims were not verified.
findings.evidence: npm's authoritative downloads API returned verbatim {downloads:97332, ...package:'skybridge'} and {downloads:52929, ...package:'create-skybridge'} for the identical window. Package identity confirmed as the Alpic framework (skybridge@1.1.2 homepage github.com/alpic-ai/skybridge, depends on @modelcontextprotocol/ext-apps + react). 52,929 = 52.9% of 100K ('roughly half'); 100,000−97,332 = 2.67% gap ('within ~3%'). Skeptical reading: 90-day totals (skybridge 296,955 vs create-skybridge 146,675) and weekday/weekend seasonality indicate CI/transitive inflation, so the scaffolder count is the stronger adoption signal. Single 30-day snapshot, not a sustained average. The '10% of MCP apps' and 'stars' marketing figures were explicitly outside the verified set.

findings.claim: Concrete fit for genie: a two-surface app (live component preview grid + UI-kit file browser) with refine/generate flows would benefit materially from Skybridge — but adoption should be partial and guarded. Gains: type-safe tool→view binding maps cleanly onto generate/refine round-trips; DevTools + --tunnel let genie test the preview/browser surfaces against real MCP clients before shipping; MIT license + optional self-hosting avoid hard lock-in; and because Skybridge sits atop the standardized ui://+iframe primitives, genie keeps a low-cost ejection path to raw SDK or mcp-ui. Risks/losses: genie's 'harness-agnostic' identity leans on cross-harness parity that no party guarantees and that Skybridge's docs do not demonstrate beyond ChatGPT/Claude/VSCode; embedded-tier CSP handling and inline/fullscreen/pip parity are unproven in the evidence.
findings.evidence: This finding is an inferential synthesis, not a single sourced claim. It rests on confirmed facts: Skybridge's type-safe binding + devtools/tunnel (Finding 1), low lock-in because it is sugar over a standard (Finding 3), the unguaranteed cross-harness parity (Finding 2), and the vendor-neutral mcp-ui fallback (Finding 4). The recommendation — prototype genie on Skybridge for velocity while preserving the ui://+iframe escape hatch and independently validating Cursor/VS Code/Goose parity and embedded-tier CSP — follows directly. Marked medium confidence because the genie-specific payoff is reasoned from the architecture, not measured, and key constraints (CSP, display modes, real multi-harness parity) remain unverified.

caveats: Source quality was high overall — nearly every confirmed claim rests on primary sources (official MCP spec blog/repo PR #1865, OpenAI Apps SDK docs, Skybridge's own docs/GitHub, npm's downloads API, mcp-ui repo/site). Key weaknesses and time-sensitivity: (1) The WebSearch tool was degraded/non-functional across most verifications, so independent external corroboration is thin; conclusions lean on (authoritative) primary fetches and self-descriptions. (2) Several question-critical items are UNADDRESSED by any confirmed claim and must be treated as unknown, not negative: embedded-tier CSP handling (`default-src none`, no web fonts), and whether Skybridge supports all MCP Apps display modes (inline/fullscreen/pip) — refuted/eliminated claims suggest its docs name only 'inline'/'fullscreen' examples. (3) Cross-harness reach is weaker than marketing implies: refuted claims show Skybridge's docs name only ChatGPT, Claude, and VSCode (plus 'any MCP client') — Cursor and Goose are not named. (4) Marketing figures '~10% of MCP apps' and '1K+ GitHub stars' were NOT verified (claim [12] explicitly excluded them). (5) npm figures are a single 30-day snapshot (ends 2026-06-21) inflated by CI/transitive installs, not a sustained or human-only adoption measure. (6) Time-sensitivity: MCP Apps reached 'Final' status only Jan 28 2026 and is intentionally lean/expanding; mcp-ui's 'production-ready' is self-asserted with a use-at-your-own-risk disclaimer; Skybridge self-host steps are deferred to an external guide and were not independently exercised.
openQuestions: Does Skybridge actually deliver working parity inside Cursor, VS Code, and Goose? Its docs name only ChatGPT/Claude/VSCode, and the MCP Apps spec guarantees no parity — genie's harness-agnostic claim needs hands-on validation per harness.

How does Skybridge handle the embedded-tier CSP constraints (default-src none, no web fonts) and the inline/fullscreen/pip display modes? No confirmed evidence addresses these, yet they are central to genie's preview-grid + file-browser surfaces.
Are the marketing claims 'powering ~10% of MCP apps' and '1K+ GitHub stars' accurate? Neither survived verification; the substantiated signal is ~53K scaffolder runs/month, not app-share or stars.

What is the practical completeness and operational cost of self-hosting Skybridge off Alpic? The README asserts Node.js self-hosting but defers steps to an external guide, and Alpic's premium add-ons (analytics, tunneling, app-store compliance) may carry hidden feature/ops caveats.

refuted.claim: Skybridge binds an MCP server tool to a React view declaratively (e.g. `view: { component: "greeting" }`), and the view reads the tool's typed output via a generated hook `useToolInfo<"greeting">()` whose helpers are auto-generated from the server schema — but the docs never describe this as 'tRPC' or tRPC-style.
Alpic-hosted deployment is NOT mandatory: `npm run deploy` can target Alpic Cloud, Cloudflare Workers, or any Node.js-compatible/self-hosted infrastructure.

The documentation's cross-harness coverage is narrow: it names only ChatGPT and Claude (plus 'any MCP client'), with no mention of Cursor, VS Code, or Goose, and contains no explicit 'write once, run everywhere' claim; only 'inline' rendering is named, with fullscreen/pip display modes unspecified.

OpenAI's official Apps SDK docs reference Skybridge by name as the interop layer for host-backed navigation, confirming Skybridge actually works inside ChatGPT's MCP Apps host (not just Claude/self-hosted).

mcp-ui's architecture is the same hand-rolled primitive genie's current plan considers: ui:// resources rendered inside a sandboxed iframe, which addresses the embedded-tier isolation/CSP concern at the renderer level.

MCP Apps is an optional, backward-compatible extension to the core MCP protocol that must be explicitly negotiated between client and server, so building on it does not break existing implementations — a maturity/risk signal for any framework (e.g. Skybridge) that depends on it.
Skybridge's npm package is published under the ISC license, not MIT as asserted in the research question's framing.

On npm, Skybridge self-describes as a framework for building ChatGPT apps rather than a general MCP/cross-harness framework, and 'mcp' does not appear in its keywords (which are chatgpt, app, ai, skybridge).

Skybridge's cross-harness claim explicitly names only Claude, ChatGPT, and VSCode plus a generic 'compatible client' catch-all; Cursor and Goose (which the research question asks about) are not named, and there is no description of inline/pip MCP Apps display modes — only fullscreen appears in examples.
